body:
## UX goal
Give writers a fast, low-friction timeline workspace that catches continuity errors early while staying secondary to drafting (assistive, not punitive).

## Information hierarchy
1. Timeline mode access from the existing story view-mode control.
2. Top summary strip: scene count, conflict count, and one-line guidance.
3. Main timeline list ordered by chronological metadata (not manuscript order).
4. Per-scene row details: time marker, scene title/chapter, status, conflict badge, jump-to-scene action.
5. Secondary controls: metadata edit affordance and light filters (all/conflicts only, storyline tag).

## Layout and components
- Add a third mode, `timeline`, to the existing `ViewModeSwitch` so switching remains one click.
- Keep current story page shell/header pattern from `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` and render timeline as an alternate body like editor/corkboard.
- Timeline body should use a two-zone layout:
  - Sticky utility bar (summary + filters) matching corkboard toolbar density.
  - Scrollable vertical timeline list with compact rows (denser than corkboard cards).
- Each timeline row should contain:
  - Chronology marker (date/time or relative index),
  - Scene identity (title + chapter),
  - Status pill/select reuse,
  - Conflict indicator with short message,
  - Primary CTA: “Open scene”.
- Metadata editing should be inline and lightweight (single-row expand/collapse or popover), not modal-first.

## Interaction states
- Idle: sorted timeline list visible with subtle divider lines and clear chronology markers.
- Loading: skeleton rows in timeline shape (not card skeletons), keep toolbar visible.
- Empty: writer-facing prompt to add first timeline metadata with one-click jump to first scene.
- Error: inline non-blocking banner in timeline panel; preserve existing data view if available.
- Success: after metadata save/reorder conflict resolution, show brief inline confirmation and recompute ordering/conflicts immediately.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: full tab flow through mode switch, filters, row actions, and metadata inputs; Enter/Space activates row open action.
- Focus: use existing strong focus ring treatment (`--color-carbon-600`) for interactive row controls.
- Contrast: keep all text/borders within existing grayscale token contrast; conflict badges must meet 4.5:1 using token combinations.
- Text clarity: conflict copy must be deterministic and actionable (e.g., “Scene order conflict in storyline: Detective Arc”).

## Visual style
- Follow current grayscale-only system from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- Base surfaces: `--color-white` for rows, `--color-polar-100` for panel background, `--color-fashion-300` for dividers.
- Primary text hierarchy: `--color-black-700` (titles), `--color-elephant-500` (supporting meta), `--color-cement-400` (hints).
- Keep tone minimal and editorial; avoid colorful alert styling. Use weight, border, and iconography for emphasis.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop: timeline list occupies main story canvas width; utility bar stays sticky.
- Tablet: keep timeline list single-column; reduce metadata columns into stacked row meta.
- Mobile: prioritize scanability:
  - toolbar wraps into two rows,
  - row content stacks (marker -> title/meta -> actions),
  - primary action remains visible without horizontal scroll.

## Codebase alignment
Reviewed and aligned to existing primitives/routes:
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`
- `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`
- `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx`
- `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`
- `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.module.css`
- `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`
- `src/lib/scenes.ts`
- `src/types/scene.ts`
- `src/styles/tokens.css`
Design assumes timeline mode is implemented as another in-page view mode, not a separate route.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - App/story shell and header structure,
  - `ViewModeSwitch` interaction model,
  - toolbar conventions from corkboard,
  - scene status control pattern.
- New build:
  - timeline panel/list component(s),
  - timeline row subcomponent,
  - conflict badge/message treatment,
  - timeline-specific loading/empty/error UI.

## Feasibility notes
- Current scene model (`src/types/scene.ts`) has no timeline metadata fields; timeline UI requires extending data shape (e.g., timestamp or relative index + optional storyline tag).
- Current persistence in `src/lib/scenes.ts` is localStorage and currently sorted by `order`; timeline sort must be computed separately from manuscript order to satisfy acceptance criteria.
- Conflict rules should be deterministic and local-first for MVP (no AI dependency).

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Preserve quick switching parity: mode switch must remain one-click with no full-page navigation.
- Keep drafting flow primary: timeline should never block editor/corkboard usage when conflicts exist.
- File/component anchors for implementation planning:
  - `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx` (add timeline option)
  - `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` (render timeline mode + handlers)
  - `src/lib/scenes.ts` and `src/types/scene.ts` (timeline metadata persistence/schema)
  - new timeline UI module alongside existing story view components.

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
Timeline support can be a strong differentiator if we keep it practical: continuity guardrails that catch contradictions early without forcing writers into heavyweight planning software.

## Competitor evidence
- Living Writer positions structure support for long-form drafting, and chronology management is a common pain adjacent to that workflow: https://livingwriter.com/features
- Campfire’s broader worldbuilding/planning ecosystem shows demand for continuity tooling, but complexity can be high for MVP-focused writers: https://www.campfirewriting.com/
- Inkitt’s core value is reader/publishing network effects, leaving room for us to own internal continuity quality tools during drafting: https://writer.inkitt.com

## Risks / trade-offs
- Overly strict conflict rules can frustrate creative drafting.
- Timeline metadata entry can feel like administrative burden.
- Poor visualization can make the feature look powerful but feel unusable.

## Recommendation
Approve. Require design to keep timeline optional and conflict messaging assistive (not punitive), with one-click jump-to-fix from warnings.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design
